metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete grep-based proving command for each content AC (e.g., `grep -c 'Two writers, one store' docs/architecture.md`, `grep -l 'Two writers, one store' src/rebar/_store/event_append.py src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py`) so that every content-presence criterion has verifiable evidence of completion.
findings.finding: ACs 1–9 (section content and docstring additions) have no concrete proving command. The only stated proving commands are `make lint` and `make typecheck`, which pass regardless of whether the required content was added, leaving 9 of 11 criteria unverifiable by the plan's own evidence.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add a verification block after the ACs, e.g.:
```
## Verification commands
grep -c 'Two writers, one store' docs/architecture.md          # must be ≥ 1
grep -c 'applier._apply_batch' docs/architecture.md            # must be ≥ 1
grep -c 'stage_and_commit' docs/architecture.md                # must be ≥ 1
grep -rn 'Two writers, one store' \
  src/rebar/_store/event_append.py \
  src/rebar/_io/import_ndjson.py \
  src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/inbound_translate.py \
  src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/applier.py                # all four must match
```
These commands exercise the content claims directly, unlike `make lint`/`make typecheck`.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: Destructive/irreversible operation(s) with no stated safeguard.
findings.suggested_fix: State the safeguard (backup, dry-run, reversible migration, rollback) or justify the irreversibility explicitly (T4).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's canonical-serializer framing: `_write_event_file` calls `canonical_str` (not `canonical_bytes`); the 'Two writers, one store' section should state that both writers share the same *logical* canonical contract (sorted keys, compact separators, no trailing newline) but that `_write_event_file` uses `canonical_str` while `stage_and_commit` uses `canonical_bytes` — both produce byte-identical output per the contract, but the function names differ.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_write_event_file` and `stage_and_commit` share 'the canonical-bytes contract' via `canonical_bytes` as the single canonical serializer — but `_write_event_file` actually calls `canonical_str`, not `canonical_bytes`. The plan's Why section and the documentation section it prescribes risk embedding an inaccurate cross-reference that will mislead any agent who verifies the claim against the code.
findings.location: Why section: 'inbound events are written by inbound_translate._write_event_file... the canonical-bytes contract'; Scope: section to document 'the two writers share... the canonical-bytes contract'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Two writers, one store' section, replace 'share the canonical-bytes contract (canonical_bytes)' with something like: 'share the same canonical-bytes contract — sorted keys, compact separators, no trailing newline — but through different helpers: stage_and_commit uses canonical_bytes; _write_event_file uses canonical_str. Both are in rebar._store.canonical and produce byte-identical output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Refine the `_apply_batch` clarification in the 'Two writers, one store' section to state that `_apply_batch` is the outbound Jira REST sequencer whose only local-store write is a **pass-record** commit (not a ticket-event batch commit via `stage_and_commit`), so agents are not misled into thinking it makes zero local git writes.
findings.finding: _apply_batch is characterized in the plan as purely 'an OUTBOUND Jira REST sequencer (not a local commit-batcher)' but it also writes a pass-record commit to the local git store (via _write_pass_record → concurrency.rebase_retry). The planned 'Two writers, one store' section risks replacing one misconception with another: agents may conclude _apply_batch makes zero local-store writes, then be confused when they see the rebase_retry/pass-record commit in the code.
findings.location: Plan 'Why' section and AC line: 'The section clears up `applier._apply_batch` as an OUTBOUND Jira REST sequencer, not a local commit-batcher.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the section, change the `_apply_batch` description to: '`applier._apply_batch` is an OUTBOUND Jira REST sequencer — it dispatches mutations to Jira via `create_one`/`update_one`/`delete_one`. Its one local-store write is a pass-record commit (`_write_pass_record`), not a ticket-event batch commit; it never calls `stage_and_commit` or writes ticket event files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Scope the `_write_event_file` clarification to the *function level*: state explicitly that the function itself does not call `git add`/`commit` (unlike `stage_and_commit`), and note that the reconciler's pass-level commit in `reconcile.py` is what eventually commits inbound event files to the git store — so readers don't infer inbound events are never durably committed.
findings.finding: The plan directs the doc section to state that `inbound_translate._write_event_file` writes 'with no `git add`/`commit`' — which is literally true of that function — but the reconciler DOES eventually commit inbound event files at the pass level (via `reconcile.py`'s `_commit_binding_store_snapshot` and related batch-commit machinery). Without explicit scoping to the function boundary in the planned prose, the corrective documentation risks introducing a second misunderstanding: an agent may conclude inbound events written by `_write_event_file` are never durably committed to the git store, which is wrong.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The section clears up `inbound_translate._write_event_file` as writing one event file per call with no `git add`/`commit` (not via `stage_and_commit`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the planned 'Two writers, one store' section, change the `_write_event_file` description to: '`inbound_translate._write_event_file` writes ONE event file per call using `os.replace` under the lock — it does NOT call `stage_and_commit` or invoke `git add`/`commit` itself. Inbound event files are committed to git as part of the reconciler pass-level commit in `reconcile.py`, not inline per-file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC lines 3 and 4 to specify the positive characterization only (what each function IS and does), deferring any 'see also' cross-reference to the overloaded-vocabulary list rather than co-locating negations with the false-friend identifiers.
findings.finding: AC lines 3 and 4 specify the required documentation content in negation-first / pink-elephant framing ('not a local commit-batcher,' 'with no git add/commit (not via stage_and_commit)'), mandating that the produced agent-facing section leads with the wrong concept before correcting it. Because the plan's stated purpose is to stop agents from being misled by these exact false friends, mandating negation-heavy phrasing in the AC perpetuates the instruction-locality antipattern: an agent reading the resulting section will still process 'batch commit' and 'stage_and_commit' as salient concepts co-located with _apply_batch and _write_event_file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines 3 and 4: '[ ] The section clears up applier._apply_batch as an OUTBOUND Jira REST sequencer, not a local commit-batcher.' and '[ ] The section clears up inbound_translate._write_event_file as writing one event file per call with no git add/commit (not via stage_and_commit).'
findings.suggested_fix: AC line 3: '[ ] The section characterizes applier._apply_batch as an outbound Jira REST sequencer that dispatches mutations to the Jira API, with no local store writes, and cross-references the overloaded-vocabulary list for "batch".' AC line 4: '[ ] The section characterizes inbound_translate._write_event_file as writing one event file per call under the write lock via os.replace, and cross-references event_append.stage_and_commit in the overloaded-vocabulary list to explain why the two are distinct.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
